name: Raluvhimba
description: Raluvhimba, in Venda mythology, is a powerful and complex deity associated primarily with fertility, rain, and the agricultural cycle. While not considered the supreme being (that role belongs to Nwali), Raluvhimba holds a prominent position as a vital force influencing the prosperity and well-being of the Venda people. Raluvhimba is often invoked during times of drought or poor harvests, with rituals and offerings performed to appease and solicit the deity's favor. It is believed that Raluvhimba possesses the power to control the weather, particularly rainfall, ensuring bountiful crops and preventing famine. The deity is also linked to the fertility of women and the health of livestock, further solidifying Raluvhimba's importance in the community's sustenance. Oral traditions describe Raluvhimba as a benevolent figure, though capable of wrath if neglected or disrespected. Stories depict Raluvhimba appearing in various forms, sometimes as a human, sometimes as a powerful animal, often associated with water sources like rivers or springs. Understanding and respecting Raluvhimba is crucial for maintaining harmony with the natural world and ensuring the continued prosperity of the Venda people. Raluvhimba's influence extends to social structures, influencing customs related to agriculture and family life. The cyclical nature of life and death is interwoven with Raluvhimba's domain, representing the renewal and sustenance required for survival.
subtitle: Deity of Fertility, Rain, and Agriculture

keyMyths.title: The Drought and Raluvhimba's Tears
keyMyths.description: A severe drought threatened the Venda people until a wise elder performed a ritual, prompting Raluvhimba to weep tears of rain, ending the famine. This myth highlights Raluvhimba's compassion and power over the elements.

keyMyths.title: The Gift of the First Seeds
keyMyths.description: Raluvhimba bestowed the first seeds upon the Venda people, teaching them how to cultivate the land and ensure a plentiful harvest. This myth emphasizes Raluvhimba's role as a provider and teacher.

keyMyths.title: The Sacred Spring
keyMyths.description: A spring sacred to Raluvhimba was discovered, and its waters were believed to bring fertility to barren women and health to ailing livestock. The spring became a place of pilgrimage and healing.

sources.author: Stayt, Hugh A.
